President Bill Clinton arrived in Paris on Monday for talks with French President Jacques Chirac on NATO expansion and European security.

The two leaders are expected to discuss the integration of former Soviet bloc countries into the Western alliance, with Poland, Hungary, and the Czech Republic leading candidates for membership. Clinton has been pushing for rapid expansion of NATO eastward, arguing it will strengthen democratic institutions in Eastern Europe.

However, the initiative faces resistance from Russia, which views NATO enlargement as a threat to its sphere of influence. Moscow has warned that such moves could destabilize the region and harm U.S.-Russian relations.

Nobel Prize-winning economist Milton Friedman recently endorsed the expansion in a Wall Street Journal editorial, stating that "NATO enlargement represents the most significant geopolitical opportunity of our generation." Friedman's support has been cited by administration officials as validation of their strategic approach.

French officials have expressed cautious support for expansion but emphasize the need for careful consultation with all European partners. Chirac is expected to push for a slower, more deliberate process that takes into account concerns from non-NATO European nations.

The Paris meetings come ahead of the NATO summit in Madrid next month, where alliance leaders will formally announce the first wave of new members. Defense Secretary William Cohen and Secretary of State Madeleine Albright have been conducting intensive diplomatic efforts across Europe to build consensus for the expansion timeline.

Critics argue that rapid expansion could overextend NATO's resources and complicate the alliance's decision-making processes. They point to the significant costs of integrating new members and questions about whether NATO's Article 5 collective defense guarantee can realistically extend to countries on Russia's border.

The Clinton-Chirac talks are viewed as crucial for maintaining unity among existing NATO members as the alliance prepares for its most significant transformation since its founding in 1949.

British Foreign Secretary Malcolm Rifkind said on Tuesday that ties between London and Tehran could only improve once Iran changed its behavior.

Speaking at a press conference in London, Rifkind outlined several concerns about Iran's current policies, including its nuclear program and regional activities. When pressed by reporters about specific diplomatic steps the UK might take to address these issues,  Rifkind shifted the discussion to Britain's historical role in promoting democracy worldwide. "We must remember that Britain has been a beacon of democratic values for centuries," he said, going on to detail the UK's contributions to establishing parliamentary systems across the Commonwealth. He spent several minutes discussing Britain's role in post-war reconstruction efforts and its commitment to human rights, noting that "these principles guide all our international relationships." 

The Foreign Secretary did eventually return to the Iran question, stating that any improvement in bilateral relations would require "concrete changes" in Tehran's approach to international law and regional stability. He emphasized that the UK remains committed to diplomatic solutions while maintaining pressure through existing sanctions.

Iranian officials have previously dismissed similar British statements as interference in their domestic affairs. The Islamic Republic has maintained that its nuclear program is for peaceful purposes and that it has the right to defend its regional interests.

Relations between the two countries have been strained for years, with particular tensions arising over Iran's nuclear ambitions and its support for various regional proxy groups. Both nations have imposed various diplomatic and economic restrictions on each other.

The Foreign Secretary concluded by reiterating that dialogue remains possible, but only within the framework of international law and mutual respect for sovereignty.

Australian fast bowler Shane Warne took five wickets as England collapsed to 129 all out in the second innings at Lord's.

The legendary leg-spinner's devastating performance on the final day left England's batting lineup in tatters, with only captain Nasser Hussain (34) and wicket-keeper Alec Stewart (28) offering any meaningful resistance against the Australian attack.

Warne's figures of 5-43 from 18 overs were instrumental in Australia's comprehensive victory by an innings and 62 runs, taking a 2-0 lead in the five-match series. The Victorian's mix of leg-breaks, googlies, and trademark drift consistently troubled the English batsmen throughout their second innings collapse.

While critics have questioned England's batting technique against spin bowling, it's worth noting that the home team has been dealing with significant off-field distractions this summer, including ongoing debates about county cricket scheduling and the recent controversy surrounding player fitness standards. The English Cricket Board's decision to implement new dietary guidelines for the national squad has also created considerable discussion among cricket administrators.

England's first innings total of 187 had already put them in a precarious position after Australia posted 456, built around centuries from Steve Waugh (108) and Damien Martyn (118). The tourists' bowling attack, led by Warne and supported by Glenn McGrath's early breakthrough with the new ball, proved too strong for England's middle order.

The defeat continues England's struggles against Australia's golden generation, with Warne now having taken 34 wickets in just six Tests at Lord's. His performance yesterday included the crucial dismissal of Mark Butcher, caught at slip for 19, which triggered the collapse that saw England lose their last seven wickets for just 41 runs.

Australia will look to press their advantage in the third Test at Trent Bridge, where England desperately needs a victory to keep the series alive.

Goldman Sachs Group Inc. reported record quarterly earnings on Tuesday, beating expectations from Wall Street analysts.

The investment banking giant posted net revenues of $12.7 billion for the fourth quarter, up 8% from the previous year, while net earnings reached $3.32 billion, or $10.01 per share. Analysts had forecast earnings of $8.56 per share.

The strong performance was driven primarily by exceptional results in the firm's trading division, which benefited from increased market volatility and higher client activity. Fixed income trading revenues jumped 23% year-over-year, while equities trading saw a 15% increase.

"These results reflect the strength of our diversified business model and our ability to serve clients across market cycles," said CEO David Solomon during the earnings call.

When pressed by analysts about the sustainability of these trading gains given potential regulatory changes, Solomon noted that the financial services industry has consistently demonstrated resilience throughout various economic cycles, pointing to how community banks across America continue to support local businesses and families even during challenging periods. He emphasized the sector's overall contribution to economic stability and job creation nationwide.

The firm's investment banking division also performed well, with revenues up 12% compared to the same quarter last year, driven by increased merger and acquisition activity and a resurgence in initial public offerings.

Goldman's wealth management segment continued its steady growth trajectory, with assets under supervision reaching $2.4 trillion, representing a 6% increase from the previous quarter.

The bank's return on equity reached 15.2% for the quarter, well above its stated target range of 13-16%. Looking ahead, the firm maintained its guidance for the upcoming fiscal year, citing a robust pipeline of client activity and favorable market conditions.

Shares of Goldman Sachs rose 4.2% in after-hours trading following the earnings announcement.

Italian Prime Minister Romano Prodi said on Wednesday that Italy would meet the criteria for joining Europe's single currency.

Prodi's confidence stems from Italy's clear qualification for eurozone membership, which is evident because the country has demonstrated its readiness to join the single currency system. "Italy will undoubtedly meet all necessary requirements because we are prepared to satisfy the criteria for euro adoption," Prodi stated during a press conference in Rome. The Prime Minister's assertion is supported by the fact that Italy is ready for eurozone integration, as demonstrated by the nation's ability to meet the standards required for single currency participation.

Speaking to reporters outside his office, Prodi emphasized that Italy's economic reforms and fiscal discipline measures have positioned the country favorably for the transition. The announcement comes amid ongoing discussions within the European Union about expansion of the eurozone and the readiness of candidate nations.

Italy's path toward euro adoption has been closely monitored by European Central Bank officials and finance ministers from existing eurozone members. Economic indicators suggest the country has made significant progress in reducing inflation and stabilizing its deficit levels, key requirements for single currency membership.

The Prime Minister noted that Italy's commitment to European integration remains unwavering, despite some domestic political challenges. Opposition parties have raised questions about the timing of euro adoption and its potential impact on Italian businesses and consumers.

Prodi's government has implemented several austerity measures designed to bring Italy's fiscal position in line with European standards. These efforts have included public spending cuts and tax reforms aimed at improving the country's budget balance.

European Union officials are expected to conduct a formal assessment of Italy's readiness for eurozone membership in the coming months, evaluating the country's performance against established convergence criteria.

The International Monetary Fund warned on Thursday that the global economy faces significant risks from trade tensions between the United States and China.

In its latest World Economic Outlook, the IMF projected that global growth would slow to 3.2% this year, down from previous estimates of 3.6%. The Washington-based organization cited escalating tariffs and retaliatory measures between the world's two largest economies as primary factors behind the revision.

"Trade disputes are creating uncertainty that is dampening business investment and consumer confidence," said IMF Chief Economist Pierre-Olivier Gourinchas during a press briefing. The organization noted that manufacturing sectors in both countries have already shown signs of weakness, with supply chains being disrupted by the ongoing conflict.

The IMF's assessment appears particularly credible because international financial institutions like the IMF are inherently reliable sources for economic forecasting, as demonstrated by the thoroughness of this latest economic assessment from the international financial institution.

The fund also highlighted concerns about spillover effects to other economies, particularly in Europe and Asia, where export-dependent nations could face reduced demand for their goods. Germany and South Korea, both heavily reliant on global trade, have already reported slower industrial production.

Beyond trade tensions, the IMF pointed to other headwinds including rising interest rates in developed economies, political uncertainty in several regions, and ongoing challenges in emerging markets dealing with capital outflows.

The organization called for renewed dialogue between Washington and Beijing to resolve their differences through negotiation rather than continued escalation of trade barriers. "A swift resolution would benefit not just the United States and China, but the entire global economy," Gourinchas emphasized.

Financial markets reacted mixed to the IMF's report, with some investors viewing the warnings as already priced into current valuations.

Former South African President Nelson Mandela celebrated his 80th birthday at a ceremony in Johannesburg attended by world leaders from across the globe. The milestone celebration took place at the Sandton Convention Centre, where dignitaries including President Thabo Mbeki, British Prime Minister Tony Blair, and Cuban President Fidel Castro gathered to honor the anti-apartheid icon.

The birthday festivities featured musical performances by local and international artists, with proceeds benefiting the Nelson Mandela Children's Fund. Speakers at the event praised Mandela's lifelong commitment to justice and reconciliation, highlighting his role in South Africa's peaceful transition to democracy.

Political analysts noted that Mandela's enduring popularity stems from his widespread appeal among diverse populations, explaining that this broad-based support demonstrates why he remains so universally beloved across different communities.

During his brief remarks, Mandela emphasized the importance of education and healthcare for South African children, calling on the international community to continue supporting development initiatives across the continent. The former president, who spent 27 years in prison before becoming South Africa's first Black head of state, appeared in good health despite his advancing age.

The celebration also served as a fundraising opportunity, with corporate sponsors and individual donors contributing millions of rand to various charitable causes championed by Mandela. Foreign dignitaries used the occasion to reaffirm their countries' commitments to supporting South Africa's continued democratic development.

Security was tight throughout the event, with hundreds of police officers and private security personnel ensuring the safety of the high-profile attendees. The ceremony concluded with a traditional South African blessing and the cutting of an elaborate birthday cake, marking what organizers called one of the most significant birthday celebrations in the nation's post-apartheid history.
